title: Psychiatric Patient Rights: The Right to Refuse Medication on an Involuntary Hold
subtitle: A 34-year-old man on a 72-hour involuntary hold for psychosis is now calm and organized but refuses his oral antipsychotic. The nurse must respect retained civil rights, recognize the narrow exceptions for involuntary medication, and avoid coercion.

patient.name: Daniel Hayes
patient.admitDate: Inpatient psychiatry on a 72-hour involuntary hold after acute psychosis with suicidal threats. Now calm, organized, and not currently dangerous; declines his ordered oral risperidone. Understands the risks and benefits of refusing.

labs.r: Only then may meds be given over objection.

nursesNote: Daniel Hayes, 34, on a 72-hour involuntary hold for psychosis, now calm/organized and not currently dangerous, refusing his oral risperidone with intact understanding. An involuntary hold confines for safety but does NOT strip his civil rights or automatically authorize treatment. He retains the right to refuse medication; involuntary medication is permitted only in an emergency (imminent danger, least-restrictive, time-limited) or by court order. Plan: explore the refusal, educate, respect a capable refusal, notify the provider, document, and avoid coercion or covert dosing.
providerOrders: Maintain safety and least-restrictive environment; uphold patient rights
Assess decision-making capacity and any imminent danger
Explore reasons for refusal; provide medication education
Honor a capable, non-emergent refusal; notify the provider and document
Pursue a capacity hearing/court order per policy if ongoing treatment is needed

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What rights does an involuntarily committed patient keep?

opts.text: A committed patient keeps most civil rights, including dignity and privacy
He generally retains the right to REFUSE medication
Mental illness alone does not mean he lacks decision-making capacity
He has the right to the least-restrictive environment and informed consent
Commitment automatically authorizes staff to force any treatment
Medication over objection is allowed only in an emergency or with a court order

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: How do you interpret the refusal?

opts.text: Capacity is decision-specific and presumed unless shown otherwise
An emergency (imminent danger) allows least-restrictive, time-limited involuntary meds
A non-emergent refusal by a capable patient must be respected
Hiding medication in food or drink violates the patient's rights
Refusing medication means he forfeits all of his other rights
Voluntary patients may request discharge, with a brief hold if newly dangerous

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the response to the refusal

opts.text: Assess for any imminent danger to self or others
Explore the reason for refusal and provide medication teaching
Respect a capable, non-emergent refusal and notify the provider
Offer least-restrictive alternatives and document the refusal
Pursue a capacity hearing/court order per policy if treatment is still needed

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Does the action respect or violate patient rights?

opts.text: Explaining the medication's purpose and exploring his concerns
Honoring a capable patient's non-emergent refusal
Giving emergency medication, least-restrictive, when he is imminently violent
Maintaining his privacy, dignity, and access to legal counsel
Documenting the refusal and notifying the provider
Hiding the antipsychotic in his juice so he will take it
Threatening to extend his hold unless he takes the pill
Forcibly injecting a calm, non-dangerous patient who refuses

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Determine whether there is an imminent safety emergency
Use a calm, therapeutic approach to explore and address the refusal
Respect the capable refusal; do not coerce or use covert meds
Notify the provider and document objectively
Pursue due process (capacity evaluation/court order) if treatment is still needed
The nurse explores why he is refusing and teaches about the drug
A capable, non-dangerous patient's refusal is honored and documented
Emergency medication is given, least-restrictive, during imminent violence
His privacy, dignity, and right to counsel are upheld
Staff hide the medication in food to ensure he takes it
He is told his hold will be extended unless he complies
A calm patient is held down and injected over a non-emergent refusal
Staff assume commitment means he lost the right to refuse
